food_subgroup: Rice bowl dish with grilled freshwater eel

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Unagi Don (grilled eel rice bowl). FOODON_03311737 (eel food product) is used as the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKW (Eel, grilled) is used as the closest parent; no composite Unagi Don dish code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Unagi Don or grilled eel rice bowl as a composite dish.

main_ingredients: tare sauce (soy sauce, mirin, sake, sugar)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Unagi Don is approximately 380 g, comprising a bowl of steamed Japanese rice topped with one or two fillets of grilled freshwater eel glazed with tare sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Unagi (eel) kabayaki and cooked Japanese rice — composite estimate from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Eel, mixed species, cooked, dry heat (NDB 15030) and Rice, white, short-grain, cooked (NDB 20050)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Unagi Don is a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for cooked eel (dry heat) and cooked short-grain white rice, combined in a ratio of approximately 40% eel (kabayaki-style, with tare glaze adding sugar and sodium) and 60% cooked rice by weight. Tare sauce contribution to sodium and sugar is incorporated based on standard kabayaki glaze composition. No single approved-source entry for Unagi Don as a composite dish was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice, Japanese short-grain, cooked (GI ~54); eel component contributes negligible GI; composite dish GI estimated from rice-dominant carbohydrate fraction
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Unagi Don as a composite dish. GI is estimated at 54 based on the dominant carbohydrate source being Japanese short-grain white rice (GI ~54 per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables). The eel and tare sauce components contribute protein, fat, and sugar but do not materially alter the overall GI given the rice-dominant carbohydrate fraction. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (54 × 81.7) / 100 ≈ 44.1, where carbohydrate per 380 g serving = 21.5 × 3.8 = 81.7 g. gi_category set to low per validator threshold (GI ≤ 55 = low).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from tare sauce glaze
hypertension.guidance: A single serving of Unagi Don contributes approximately 1,178 mg sodium (310 mg per 100 g × 380 g), representing roughly 51% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-tare preparations or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load from white rice base and added sugar in tare sauce
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic load of approximately 44 per serving and added sugars from the tare glaze, Unagi Don poses a moderate postprandial glucose challenge for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; portion control, substitution with brown rice where available, and pairing with non-starchy vegetables are recommended strategies.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Favourable omega-3 fatty acid profile from freshwater eel partially offset by moderate sodium and cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Freshwater eel provides omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids and vitamin D, which are cardioprotective; however, the moderate sodium load (~1,178 mg per serving) and cholesterol content (~198 mg per serving) warrant mindful consumption for individuals with established cardiovascular disease, ideally within a broader heart-healthy dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Unagi Don (うなぎ丼, also called Unadon) is a classic Japanese rice bowl dish consisting of steamed short-grain Japanese rice topped with one or more fillets of freshwater eel (unagi, Anguilla japonica) that have been grilled and glazed with a sweet-savoury tare sauce made from soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar. The preparation traditionally follows the kabayaki technique: the eel is butterflied, skewered, grilled over charcoal, briefly steamed to render fat and soften the flesh, then re-grilled and repeatedly basted with tare sauce to build a lacquered, caramelised glaze. The result is tender, moist eel with a slightly smoky, deeply umami flavour and a glossy, sweet coating. Sansho pepper is a traditional garnish that adds a citrusy, numbing counterpoint. Nutritionally, Unagi Don is a moderate-energy dish (~168 kcal per 100 g) providing meaningful protein (~9.8 g/100 g), omega-3 fatty acids, vitamin D (~4.2 mcg/100 g), and vitamin B12 (~1.8 mcg/100 g). The primary clinical considerations are its moderate-to-high glycemic load driven by the white rice base, and its elevated sodium content from the tare glaze, making portion awareness important for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension. In Singapore, Unagi Don is served at Japanese restaurants and food delivery platforms such as field survey, with GyoGyo at Funan being one notable outlet. The dish is culturally associated with stamina and vitality in Japan, traditionally consumed on Doyo no Ushi no Hi (midsummer day of the ox).

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry for Unagi Don as a composite dish was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for cooked eel (dry heat) and cooked short-grain white rice in a 40:60 ratio with tare sauce sodium and sugar adjustment. GI is analogously estimated from Japanese short-grain white rice GI data in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA SR Legacy: Eel, mixed species, cooked dry heat (NDB 15030) and Rice, white, short-grain, cooked (NDB 20050), blended at approximately 40% eel and 60% rice by weight, with tare sauce contribution to sodium (~120 mg/100g increment) and sugar (~2.5 g/100g increment) incorporated. 2. trans_fat_g is set to 0.0; this is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy data for eel and rice — neither ingredient contains industrially produced trans fats. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g (-1 sentinel not used; value 2.4 g estimated from USDA eel lipid profile). 4. GI set to 54 based on analogous estimate from Japanese short-grain white rice per University of Sydney GI Database; gi_category is low per validator threshold (GI ≤ 55 = low). 5. GL per serving = (54 × 81.7) / 100 ≈ 44.1, where 81.7 g carbohydrate per 380 g serving = 21.5 g/100g × 3.8. 6. FoodOn URI FOODON_03311737 (eel food product) used as closest parent; no composite Unagi Don term exists. FoodEx2 code A0CKW (Eel, grilled) used as closest parent. 7. The restaurant name '501D Unagi Don' in the input appears to be a menu item name at GyoGyo - Funan; canonical food name normalised to 'Unagi Don'. 8. No -1 sentinel values remain unexplained; all micronutrient values are positive estimates from USDA SR Legacy composite calculation. 9. Aliases consistency verified: all strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in the flat aliases list.
